content.full_text:
August 25, 1992


Mark S. Rosen
2107 North Broadway, Suite 202
Santa Ana, CA 92706

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-435

Dear Mr. Rosen:

You have requested advice on behalf of the Santa Ana Chamber
of Commerce Political Action Committee ("SACPAC") concerning the
campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. Does SACPAC become a "slate mailer organization" by
virtue of using a mailer to endorse four candidates?

2. Is SACPAC making contributions to the candidates endorsed
in its mailer?

3. Does SACPAC's use of a consultant, who is also a
consultant for candidates endorsed in a mailer, have any effect on
the nature of SACPAC or whether the mailer is a contribution to
the candidates endorsed?


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Rosen, Mark
A-92-435
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The activities of SACPAC do not fit the definition of a
slate mailer organization.

     2. and 3. Whether SACPAC is making contributions to, or
independent expenditures for, the candidates supported in its
mailing will depend on the specific facts. In addition SACPAC may
be a controlled committee.

FACTS

     You are writing on behalf of SACPAC, which is the political
action committee of the Santa Ana Chamber of Commerce. The
committee has existed for several years endorsing candidates for
non-partisan office. SACPAC intends to put out a mailer
supporting three candidates for the Santa Ana city council and a
mayoral candidate. The mailer will be sent to voters throughout
the city of Santa Ana and it will include a solicitation for
absentee ballots. SACPAC does not intend to solicit payments from
the candidates being supported or anyone else for the specific
purpose of paying for the mailing. The cost of producing the slate
mailer will be paid from general contributions received by SACPAC.
SACPAC intends to use a consultant to help design the mailer, who
may also be a consultant for some of the candidates supported in
the mailing.

ANALYSIS

     Section 82048.4 provides that:

          (a)  "Slate mailer organization" means, except as
     provided in subdivision (b), any person who, directly or
     indirectly, does all of the following:
          (1)  Is involved in the production of one or more slate
     mailers and exercises control over the selection of the
     candidates and measures to be supported or opposed in the
     slate mailers.
          (2)  Receives or is promised payments totaling five
     hundred dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year for the
     production of one or more slate mailers.

     You have indicated that SACPAC will not receive payments
specifically for the production of a slate mailer, but will use
contributions received from its members for general campaign
purposes to pay for the mailing. Therefore, SACPAC does not fit
the definition of a "slate mailer organization."

     SACPAC must report payments for the mailing on its campaign
statement as either independent expenditures or contributions.
If SACPAC makes an expenditure in connection with a communication
that clearly identifies and expressly advocates support of a
candidate, and the expenditure is not made at the behest of that
candidate or his or her agent, it makes an independent

Rosen, Mark
A-92-435
Page 3

expenditure. If SACPAC makes an expenditure for a political
purpose and does so at the behest of the candidate, it makes a
contribution to the candidate.

Regulation 18215(b) provides that:

"Made at the behest" means a payment made under the
control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, or at the request or
suggestion of a candidate, controlled committees, official
committee of a political party, or organization formed or
existing primarily for political purposes.

You have not provided enough specific information for us to
determine whether SACPAC will be making independent expenditures
or contributions. We will be happy to provide more specific
advice if we are given more information. However, in the past we
have advised that: "... a committee's use of the same consultant
or pollster used by a candidate does not, in and of itself,
transform committee expenditures which support the candidate into
contributions. If the consultant is under contract with the
candidate and the committee at the time the expenditures are made,
there is a strong inference that the expenditures are being
coordinated by the candidate and are thus being made at his or her
behest. However, the consultant's prior employment alone does not
transform an otherwise independent expenditure into a
contribution." (Davis Advice Letter, I-90-173, copy enclosed.)

In regard to your question about whether SACPAC's use of a
consultant who is also a consultant for a candidate has any effect
on the nature of SACPAC, we cannot provide a definitive answer
without more information. The Act defines "controlled committee"
as:

...a committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by
a candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee or state measure
proponent in connection with the making of expenditures. A
candidate or state measure proponent controls a committee if
he, his agent or any other committee he controls has a
significant influence on the actions or decisions of the
committee. (Section 82016.)

The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled
committee" very broadly to include any significant participation
by a candidate, his or her agent, or representatives of any other
committee he or she controls in the actions of a committee.

To determine whether a candidate controls a committee, we
look at the degree of the candidate's involvement in the
committee's activities. The involvement of a candidate includes
the involvement of his or her campaign committee and his or her
agents. (Roberti Advice Letter, No. I-90-339, copy enclosed.)

Rosen, Mark
A-92-435
Page 4

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]
By: Wayne P. Imberi
Political Reform Consultant
